subject: history

hero.title: Arrival of Europeans in India
hero.subtitle: The transition of foreign traders into imperial rulers and the dawn of modern Indian history.
hero.whyItMatters: Core foundational topic for Modern Indian History in SSC CGL. Questions test sequence of arrival, trading factories, key governors, naval battles, and treaties.

quickFacts.heading: First European to Arrive
quickFacts.fact: Vasco da Gama reached Calicut in May 1498.

quickFacts.heading: Blue Water Policy
quickFacts.fact: Introduced by Francisco de Almeida (First Portuguese Governor).

quickFacts.heading: First Portuguese Fort
quickFacts.fact: Fort Cochin (1503) — first European fort in India.

quickFacts.heading: Goa Captured
quickFacts.fact: Alfonso de Albuquerque captured Goa from Bijapur Sultanate in 1510.

quickFacts.heading: First English Factory
quickFacts.fact: Established at Surat in 1608 by William Hawkins.

quickFacts.heading: Mughal Permission
quickFacts.fact: Thomas Roe got Mughal trading rights from Jahangir in 1615.

quickFacts.heading: Dutch in India
quickFacts.fact: Dutch East India Company (VOC) set up factory at Masulipatnam in 1605.

quickFacts.heading: French First Factory
quickFacts.fact: French set up factory at Surat in 1668, then Pondicherry in 1674.

quickFacts.heading: Battle of Swally
quickFacts.fact: 1612 — English defeated Portuguese near Surat, gaining Mughal trust.

timeline.title: Vasco da Gama reaches Calicut
timeline.shortDescription: Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama arrived at Calicut (Kozhikode) on the Malabar Coast via the Cape of Good Hope route, opening direct sea trade between Europe and India.
timeline.importance: First European sea route to India established. Zamorin of Calicut welcomed him.

timeline.title: Pedro Álvares Cabral arrives
timeline.shortDescription: Cabral led the second Portuguese expedition to India but faced hostility from Arab traders at Calicut. He then shifted focus to Cochin (Kochi).
timeline.importance: Established first Portuguese trading relations at Cochin — a rival of Calicut.

timeline.title: Vasco da Gama's Second Voyage
timeline.shortDescription: Returned with a fleet; defeated Arab and Indian merchant ships; forced Zamorin to comply with Portuguese demands.
timeline.importance: Demonstrated Portuguese naval dominance and established trade monopoly.

timeline.title: First European Fort — Fort Cochin
timeline.shortDescription: Portuguese built Fort Manuel in Cochin, marking the first permanent European fortification in India.
timeline.importance: Strategic milestone; gave Portuguese a permanent foothold on the Malabar Coast.

timeline.title: Francisco de Almeida — First Portuguese Viceroy
timeline.shortDescription: Appointed as the first Viceroy (Governor) of Portuguese India. Introduced the 'Blue Water Policy' (Cartaze system) to control Indian Ocean trade.
timeline.importance: Blue Water Policy = controlling seas rather than land. Frequent SSC MCQ.

timeline.title: Battle of Diu (First)
timeline.shortDescription: Almeida defeated the combined fleet of Egypt, Gujarat, and Calicut at Diu, establishing Portuguese naval supremacy.
timeline.importance: Decisive victory — Portuguese became undisputed masters of Indian Ocean trade.

timeline.title: Albuquerque captures Goa
timeline.shortDescription: Alfonso de Albuquerque captured Goa from the Bijapur Sultanate. Goa became the capital of Portuguese India.
timeline.importance: Goa = Portuguese HQ in India. Albuquerque = greatest Portuguese administrator.

timeline.title: Portuguese capture Malacca
timeline.shortDescription: Albuquerque captured Malacca (Malaysia), completing the triangle: Hormuz–Goa–Malacca.
timeline.importance: Established Portuguese control over the entire Eastern trade route.

timeline.title: English East India Company Founded
timeline.shortDescription: Queen Elizabeth I granted a Royal Charter to the East India Company (EIC) on 31 December 1600.
timeline.importance: Foundation of British presence in India. First Governor: Sir Thomas Smythe.

timeline.title: Dutch East India Company (VOC) Founded
timeline.shortDescription: The Vereenigde Oost-Indische Compagnie (VOC) was established in the Netherlands to challenge Portuguese and Spanish trade dominance.
timeline.importance: World's first multinational corporation and joint-stock company.

timeline.title: Dutch factory at Masulipatnam
timeline.shortDescription: Dutch set up their first factory in India at Masulipatnam (modern Andhra Pradesh).
timeline.importance: Beginning of Dutch commercial expansion into India.

timeline.title: William Hawkins arrives at Surat
timeline.shortDescription: Captain William Hawkins arrived at Surat but failed to get Jahangir's permission for a factory due to Portuguese opposition.
timeline.importance: First official English contact with Mughal court at Agra.

timeline.title: Battle of Swally (Suvali)
timeline.shortDescription: English Captain Thomas Best defeated Portuguese fleet near Surat. Impressed Jahangir, who gave English trading rights.
timeline.importance: English naval win → Mughal trust → First English factory at Surat (1613).

timeline.title: First English Factory at Surat
timeline.shortDescription: English established their first official factory at Surat after the Battle of Swally, following Jahangir's firman.
timeline.importance: Starting point of British commercial empire in India.

timeline.title: Sir Thomas Roe at Mughal Court
timeline.shortDescription: Sir Thomas Roe, as British Ambassador, negotiated with Emperor Jahangir and secured formal trading rights across the Mughal Empire.
timeline.importance: Diplomatic breakthrough — British could now trade freely throughout Mughal domains.

timeline.title: Amboyna Massacre
timeline.shortDescription: Dutch killed British traders at Amboyna (Indonesia), pushing Britain out of the Spice Islands and redirecting EIC focus to India.
timeline.importance: Pivotal — Britain permanently shifted commercial focus from Southeast Asia to India.

timeline.title: Madras Founded
timeline.shortDescription: Francis Day secured land from the Vijayanagara kingdom and founded Fort St. George at Madraspatnam — later Madras (Chennai).
timeline.importance: First permanent English settlement on India's east coast.

timeline.title: Dutch capture Malacca from Portugal
timeline.shortDescription: Dutch took Malacca from the Portuguese, marking the decline of Portuguese power and rise of Dutch commercial empire.
timeline.importance: Shift of European power dynamics in the East.

timeline.title: English factory at Hugli, Bengal
timeline.shortDescription: English established a factory at Hugli in Bengal under Gabriel Boughton, who received trade concessions from Mughal governor.
timeline.importance: Entry into Bengal — foundation for future British power in eastern India.

timeline.title: French East India Company Founded
timeline.shortDescription: Jean-Baptiste Colbert established the French East India Company (Compagnie des Indes orientales) under Louis XIV.
timeline.importance: Began French commercial and political rivalry with the British in India.

timeline.title: Bombay transferred to EIC
timeline.shortDescription: King Charles II transferred Bombay (received as dowry from Portugal) to the East India Company at £10 per year.
timeline.importance: EIC's key western headquarters. Bombay became the commercial capital of British India.

timeline.title: French establish Pondicherry
timeline.shortDescription: French established a permanent settlement at Pondicherry (Puducherry) on the Coromandel Coast under François Martin.
timeline.importance: Pondicherry became the capital of French India; remained French until 1954.

timeline.title: Calcutta Founded by Job Charnock
timeline.shortDescription: Job Charnock of the EIC established a trading post at Sutanuti on the Hooghly River — the foundation of Calcutta (Kolkata).
timeline.importance: Calcutta became the capital of British India (1772–1911). Charnock = Founder of Calcutta.

timeline.title: Dupleix becomes Governor of French India
timeline.shortDescription: Joseph François Dupleix became Governor-General of French India, aggressively pursuing a policy of political expansion and using Indian troops (sepoys).
timeline.importance: Dupleix = first to use Indian soldiers in European conflicts, a strategy later adopted by British.

timeline.title: First Carnatic War
timeline.shortDescription: Triggered by War of Austrian Succession in Europe. French captured Madras from the British.
timeline.importance: French captured Madras but returned it per Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle (1748).

timeline.title: Second Carnatic War
timeline.shortDescription: Conflict over Hyderabad and Arcot succession. Dupleix backed Muzaffar Jung; British backed Nasir Jung.
timeline.importance: Dupleix's political interference in Indian succession disputes — peak French influence.

timeline.title: Third Carnatic War begins
timeline.shortDescription: Seven Years' War (1756–63) in Europe spilled into India. British under Robert Clive vs. French under Lally.
timeline.importance: British decisively defeated French. Ended French ambitions in India.

timeline.title: Battle of Wandiwash
timeline.shortDescription: British General Sir Eyre Coote defeated French General Lally at Wandiwash (Vandavasi). This was the decisive end of French power in India.
timeline.importance: Wandiwash = Final nail in the coffin of French India. British supremacy established.

notes.heading: Portuguese in India: The Pioneers
notes.explanation: The Portuguese were the first Europeans to establish a sea route to India and create a lasting commercial and political presence. Their story is characterized by naval supremacy, the spice trade monopoly, and missionary activity.
keyTakeaways: Vasco da Gama arrived at Calicut in 1498 — the first European via sea route.
Francisco de Almeida (1505–1509): First Viceroy; introduced 'Blue Water Policy' (control seas, not land).
Alfonso de Albuquerque (1509–1515): Greatest Portuguese Governor; captured Goa (1510), Malacca (1511), Hormuz (1515).
Portuguese capital in India: GOA (captured 1510, remained Portuguese until 1961).
First European fort in India: Fort Cochin / Fort Manuel (1503).
importantExamPoints: Portuguese introduced tobacco, cashew nut, and potato to India.
Cartaze System = Portuguese permit required by all ships in the Indian Ocean.
Battle of Diu (1509) = Portuguese naval victory over Egypt + Gujarat + Calicut fleet.
Nino da Cunha shifted Portuguese capital from Cochin to Goa (1530).
Portuguese were expelled from India only in 1961 (Operation Vijay by India).
commonStudentMistakes: Confusing Almeida (Blue Water Policy) with Albuquerque (territorial expansion).
Thinking Vasco da Gama ruled India — he never served as Governor; he was just an explorer.
Confusing First Battle of Diu (1509, Portuguese vs Arab-Indian fleet) with Second Battle of Diu (1535, Portuguese allied with Gujarat vs Ottomans).

notes.heading: English East India Company: From Traders to Rulers
notes.explanation: The English East India Company (EIC) began as a trading corporation in 1600 and systematically transformed into the dominant political power in India over 258 years.
keyTakeaways: EIC founded: 31 December 1600 — Charter granted by Queen Elizabeth I.
William Hawkins (1608) — first English contact with Jahangir at Agra.
Battle of Swally (1612) — English defeated Portuguese; earned Mughal trust.
First English factory: Surat (1613) after Jahangir's firman.
Sir Thomas Roe (1615) — secured broad trading rights from Jahangir.
Bombay transferred to EIC (1668) from King Charles II — given as Portuguese dowry.
Job Charnock founded Calcutta (1690) on the Hooghly River.
importantExamPoints: Presidency towns (British India): Bombay, Madras, Calcutta — each had its own Presidency.
Fort St. George (Madras, 1639) — first English fort in India.
Fort William (Calcutta, 1700) — named after King William III.
EIC's monopoly over India trade: broken by Charter Acts of 1813 and 1833.
EIC ruled India until 1858; British Crown took over after the Revolt of 1857.
commonStudentMistakes: Thinking William Hawkins established the first factory — he failed. Thomas Best's victory (1612) led to the factory.
Confusing Fort St. George (Madras) with Fort William (Calcutta).
Thinking the EIC was a government body from the start — it was a private trading company.

notes.heading: Dutch in India: Brief but Commercially Significant
notes.explanation: The Dutch (Netherlands) established a significant commercial presence in India in the early 17th century, primarily competing with the Portuguese for the spice trade, before eventually losing influence to the British.
keyTakeaways: VOC (Dutch East India Company) founded: 1602.
First Dutch factory in India: Masulipatnam (1605).
Dutch focused on: Spices (pepper, cardamom) — primarily from Malabar Coast.
Battle of Colachel (1741): Travancore forces under Marthanda Varma defeated the Dutch — the only Asian victory over a European power in this era.
Anglo-Dutch Treaty of 1824: Dutch ceded all Indian interests to British in exchange for Sumatra.
importantExamPoints: Dutch brought the concept of joint-stock company to international trade.
Dutch had factories at: Pulicat, Surat, Chinsurah, Kasimbazar, Patna, Balasore, Nagapatnam.
Pulicat (near Chennai) was the Dutch headquarters in India.
Nagapatnam was the main Dutch port — transferred to British in 1781.
commonStudentMistakes: Thinking the Dutch were militarily powerful in India — they were primarily commercial, not territorial.
Confusing Battle of Colachel (Dutch vs Travancore, 1741) with any Carnatic War battle.

notes.heading: French in India: The Greatest Rivals to British Supremacy
notes.explanation: The French East India Company posed the most serious challenge to British dominance in India. The contest played out across three Carnatic Wars (1746–1763) before British supremacy was finally established.
keyTakeaways: French East India Company founded: 1664 by Jean-Baptiste Colbert.
First French factory: Surat (1668).
Pondicherry established: 1674 by François Martin — capital of French India.
Dupleix (1742–1754): Most ambitious French Governor; first to use Indian sepoys in inter-European conflicts.
Three Carnatic Wars: British vs French for control of South India.
Battle of Wandiwash (1761): British General Coote defeated French General Lally — end of French power.
importantExamPoints: First Carnatic War (1746–48): French captured Madras; returned per Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle.
Second Carnatic War (1749–54): Dupleix's political peak; recalled to France by French government.
Third Carnatic War (1756–63): Part of Seven Years' War. French decisively defeated.
Treaty of Paris (1763): Ended Third Carnatic War; French retained Pondicherry but lost all political power.
Pondicherry was transferred to India only in 1954 (de facto) and 1962 (de jure).
commonStudentMistakes: Confusing the three Carnatic Wars — remember: 1st = Austrian Succession, 2nd = Succession disputes, 3rd = Seven Years War.
Thinking Battle of Plassey (1757) was related to Carnatic Wars — it was in Bengal, not South India.
Confusing Dupleix (French Governor) with La Bourdonnais (French Admiral).

personalities.name: Vasco da Gama
personalities.majorContributions: Discovered sea route from Europe to India via Cape of Good Hope in 1498. Made 3 voyages to India (1497, 1502, 1524).
personalities.sscTrick: Vasco = First + Sea Route + 1498 + Calicut + Zamorin

personalities.name: Francisco de Almeida
personalities.majorContributions: Introduced the Blue Water Policy (Cartaze system) — controlling trade via naval dominance rather than territorial control. Won First Battle of Diu (1509).
personalities.sscTrick: Almeida = First Viceroy + Blue Water Policy + Battle of Diu (1509)

personalities.name: Alfonso de Albuquerque
personalities.majorContributions: Greatest Portuguese administrator. Captured Goa (1510), Malacca (1511), Hormuz (1515). Forbade Portuguese from marrying Indian women of lower status; encouraged mixed marriages.
personalities.sscTrick: Albuquerque = Real Portuguese Ruler + Goa (1510) + Triangle (Hormuz-Goa-Malacca)

personalities.name: William Hawkins
personalities.role: First English envoy to Mughal Court
personalities.majorContributions: Arrived at Surat (1608); appeared before Jahangir at Agra but failed to get factory permission due to Portuguese opposition.
personalities.sscTrick: Hawkins = First English + Surat 1608 + FAILED to get factory

personalities.name: Sir Thomas Roe
personalities.majorContributions: Appeared before Emperor Jahangir (1615–1619); negotiated formal trading rights for the EIC across the Mughal Empire.
personalities.sscTrick: Thomas Roe = British Ambassador + Jahangir + Trading Rights (1615)

personalities.name: Joseph François Dupleix
personalities.majorContributions: First European to use Indian sepoys in political warfare. Backed Indian princes in succession disputes to expand French influence. Greatest French rival to British power.

personalities.name: Robert Clive
personalities.majorContributions: Defeated French at Third Carnatic War; won Battle of Plassey (1757). Established British supremacy in India. Known as 'Clive of India'.
personalities.sscTrick: Clive = Plassey (1757) + Arcot (1751) + Start of British Empire

personalities.name: Job Charnock
personalities.majorContributions: EIC agent who established a trading post at Sutanuti (1690) on the Hooghly River — the nucleus of Calcutta.

terms.definition: Strategy introduced by Almeida: Portugal should control the Indian Ocean sea lanes (Cartaze passes) rather than building land empires, to monopolize trade.
A pass/permit issued by Portuguese authorities required by all ships sailing the Indian Ocean. Ships without a Cartaze could be seized by Portuguese warships.
Dutch East India Company (1602). World's first multinational corporation. Issued stock to raise capital for trading expeditions.
A royal decree or edict issued by a Mughal emperor. EIC obtained firmans granting trading rights from Jahangir (1613) and Farrukh Siyar (1717).
Indian soldier in European service. Dupleix was the first to use sepoys in inter-European political conflicts in India.
Three wars (1746–63) between British and French East India Companies for control of South India (Carnatic region / Deccan). British won all three.
Right to collect revenue from Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa — granted to EIC in 1765 by Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II after Battle of Buxar.
A trading post or warehouse established by a European power in India, not a manufacturing unit. The first English factory was at Surat (1613).

tables.title: European Powers: Sequence of Arrival in India
Portuguese Governors / Viceroys of India (Key for SSC)
rows: First Viceroy; Blue Water Policy; Battle of Diu
Captured Goa, Malacca, Hormuz; Greatest Portuguese ruler
Shifted capital from Cochin to Goa (1530)
Greatest Viceroy (some say); defended Diu against Ottomans

tables.title: Three Carnatic Wars: At a Glance
rows: French took Madras; returned per Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle
British won; Treaty of Paris (1763); French lost political power

memoryTricks.mnemonic: PDE-DF → Portuguese, Dutch, English, Danish, French
memoryTricks.explanation: Arrange alphabetically by 'power' order. Portugal came first (1498), Dutch (1605), English (1608), Danish (1620), French (1668).
A-A-N order for the first three key Portuguese administrators. Almeida = Blue Water. Albuquerque = Goa. Nino = shifted capital.

memoryTricks.mnemonic: MBC = Madras Before Calcutta (then Bombay joined)
memoryTricks.explanation: Madras 1639, Bombay 1668, Calcutta 1690. In SSC, remember all three and their founding figures: Francis Day, Charles II gift, Job Charnock.

memoryTricks.mnemonic: AUSE → Austrian Succession → Hyderabad Succession → European Expansion (7 Years War)
memoryTricks.explanation: 1st War = Austrian Succession War. 2nd = Indian succession disputes. 3rd = Seven Years' War. B=British always won in end.

examTraps.trap: Many students think EIC was founded in 1613 (when the first factory was set up).
examTraps.correction: EIC was founded in 1600. The first factory at Surat was established in 1613 after the Battle of Swally (1612).

examTraps.topic: First and Second Battle of Diu
examTraps.trap: Confusing the two Battles of Diu — both involving Portuguese.
examTraps.correction: First (1509): Portuguese vs Egypt + Gujarat + Calicut — Almeida's great naval victory. Second (1535): Portuguese + Gujarat vs Ottoman fleet — Portuguese won again.

examTraps.trap: Hawkins got trading rights from Jahangir — WRONG.
examTraps.correction: Hawkins FAILED. Thomas Best's victory at Swally (1612) earned Mughal trust. Thomas Roe (1615) officially negotiated trading rights.

examTraps.trap: British are credited with creating the Sepoy system.
examTraps.correction: Dupleix (French) was the FIRST to use Indian sepoys in European political conflicts. British adopted this strategy from the French.

examTraps.topic: Battle of Wandiwash vs Battle of Plassey
examTraps.trap: Treating both as the same type of battle.
examTraps.correction: Plassey (1757) = British vs Siraj-ud-Daulah (Bengal). Wandiwash (1761) = British vs French (South India). Different wars, different locations.

frequentlyTestedConcepts: Sequence of European arrival (Portuguese first in 1498)
Blue Water Policy — who introduced it and what it meant
Alfonso de Albuquerque's capture of Goa (1510)
First English factory at Surat (1613) — cause (Battle of Swally)
Carnatic Wars — causes, key battles, outcomes
Battle of Wandiwash (1761) — end of French power
repeatedThemes: What was the Blue Water Policy? (Sea control, not land)
First factory established by British EIC (Surat, 1613)
Which war ended French power? (Third Carnatic War / Wandiwash)
pyqAnalysis.difficultyTrend: Mostly direct factual questions in Tier-1. Tier-2 may ask comparative analysis (which governor introduced which policy, sequence of events).

mcqs.question: Which European power was the FIRST to establish a sea trading route to India?
mcqs.explanation: Vasco da Gama of Portugal arrived at Calicut on the Malabar Coast in May 1498, establishing the first direct sea route from Europe to India.

mcqs.question: The 'Blue Water Policy' in the context of Portuguese India was introduced by:
mcqs.explanation: Francisco de Almeida, the first Portuguese Viceroy of India (1505–1509), introduced the Blue Water Policy (Cartaze system) — a strategy of dominating trade through naval supremacy rather than land conquest.

mcqs.question: Goa was captured by the Portuguese from the Bijapur Sultanate in 1510 by:
mcqs.explanation: Alfonso de Albuquerque, the second Portuguese Governor (1509–1515), captured Goa from the Bijapur Sultanate in 1510. Goa became the capital of Portuguese India.

mcqs.question: The English East India Company was granted its Royal Charter by:
mcqs.explanation: Queen Elizabeth I granted a Royal Charter to the English East India Company on 31 December 1600, giving it the monopoly of trade with the East Indies.

mcqs.question: Which battle established English trust with the Mughal Empire and led to the first English factory at Surat?
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Swally (1612), where English Captain Thomas Best defeated the Portuguese fleet near Surat, impressed Mughal Emperor Jahangir and led to the granting of trading rights and the establishment of the first English factory at Surat in 1613.

mcqs.question: Who founded the city of Calcutta (Kolkata) for the British East India Company?
mcqs.explanation: Job Charnock, an agent of the English East India Company, established a trading post at Sutanuti on the Hooghly River in 1690, which became the foundation of Calcutta.

mcqs.question: The Battle of Wandiwash (1761) was fought between:
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Wandiwash (22 January 1761) was a decisive battle in South India where British General Sir Eyre Coote defeated French General Lally. This ended French military and political power in India.

mcqs.question: Which European power was the FIRST to use Indian sepoys in political warfare in India?
mcqs.explanation: French Governor-General Joseph François Dupleix was the first European to use Indian sepoys (soldiers) in political conflicts between Indian rulers, during the Second Carnatic War. The British later adopted this strategy.

mcqs.question: Bombay was transferred to the English East India Company from the Portuguese as part of the dowry of Catherine of Braganza in:
mcqs.explanation: Bombay was given to King Charles II of England in 1661 as part of the marriage dowry of Catherine of Braganza (Portuguese princess). Charles II leased it to the English East India Company in 1668 at £10 per year.

mcqs.question: Which treaty formally ended the Third Carnatic War and confined the French to Pondicherry without any political or military power?
mcqs.explanation: The Treaty of Paris (1763) concluded the Seven Years' War in Europe and the Third Carnatic War in India. France was allowed to keep Pondicherry but lost all political and military power in India. British supremacy was established.

flashcards.front: Which year did Vasco da Gama arrive in India, and at which port?
flashcards.back: 1498 — Calicut (Kozhikode) on the Malabar Coast. The Zamorin (local ruler) welcomed him.

flashcards.front: What was the Blue Water Policy?
flashcards.back: Strategy of Portuguese Viceroy Almeida: dominate Indian Ocean trade via naval power (Cartaze permits), not by building land empires.

flashcards.front: Who captured Goa for the Portuguese and in which year?
flashcards.back: Alfonso de Albuquerque captured Goa from the Bijapur Sultanate in 1510.

flashcards.front: When was the English East India Company founded and by whom?
flashcards.back: 31 December 1600 — Royal Charter granted by Queen Elizabeth I.

flashcards.front: What caused the establishment of the first English factory at Surat?
flashcards.back: Battle of Swally (1612) — English Captain Thomas Best defeated Portuguese fleet; Jahangir gave trading rights → Surat factory 1613.

flashcards.front: Who was the British diplomat who formally got trading rights from Jahangir?
flashcards.back: Sir Thomas Roe — British Ambassador at Mughal Court (1615–1619).

flashcards.front: Who founded Calcutta and in which year?
flashcards.back: Job Charnock founded Calcutta in 1690 at Sutanuti on the Hooghly River.

flashcards.front: Who introduced the use of Indian sepoys in European political warfare?
flashcards.back: Dupleix (French Governor-General) — first used Indian soldiers in succession dispute conflicts.

flashcards.front: What was the Battle of Wandiwash (1761)?
flashcards.back: British General Eyre Coote defeated French General Lally in South India — ended French power in India.

flashcards.front: Order of European arrivals in India: Portuguese, Dutch, English, Danish, French — with years?
flashcards.back: Portuguese (1498) → Dutch (1605) → English (1608) → Danish (1620) → French (1668). Mnemonic: PDE-DF

revisionPoints: Almeida: 1st Portuguese Viceroy → Blue Water Policy → Battle of Diu (1509)
Albuquerque: Greatest Portuguese → Goa (1510) → Malacca (1511) → Hormuz (1515)
EIC founded: 31 Dec 1600 → Queen Elizabeth I
Battle of Swally: 1612 → English vs Portuguese → Led to Surat factory (1613)
Thomas Roe: British Ambassador → Jahangir → Trading rights (1615)
Bombay: Portuguese dowry → Charles II (1661) → Leased to EIC (1668)
VOC: Dutch East India Company → 1602 → First factory Masulipatnam (1605)
French EIC: 1664 → Pondicherry (1674) → Dupleix = Sepoy Strategy
Three Carnatic Wars: 1746–48, 1749–54, 1756–63 → British won all
Battle of Wandiwash: 1761 → End of French power in India
Treaty of Paris (1763): Ended Third Carnatic War → British supreme in India
revisionSheet: 1498: Vasco da Gama → Calicut (First European to India by sea)
1505: Almeida → First Portuguese Viceroy + Blue Water Policy
1509: Battle of Diu → Portuguese naval supremacy
1608: William Hawkins → Surat (FAILED to get factory)
1612: Battle of Swally → English beat Portuguese
1615: Thomas Roe → Trading rights from Jahangir
1639: Francis Day → Fort St. George → Madras
1746–63: Three Carnatic Wars → British won all
1761: Battle of Wandiwash → End of French power
